comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on the technical and artistic aspects of the dithering technique used in the game Return of the Obra Dinn, with several users sharing resources, personal experiences, and critiques of the visual style. Some appreciate the innovation and nostalgic value, while others find the visual style detracts from gameplay enjoyment.

comments.author: abetusk
comments.text:
I keep feeling like there's a set of fundamental assumptions that can be optimized for, or relaxed and optimized for, in order to get at what a better method might be.

For example, stability of dithering under rotation and or some type of shear translation. What about stability under scaling?

There's been some other methods that essentially create a dither texture on the surface itself but, to me at least, this has a different quality than the "screen space" dithering that Obra Dinn employs.

Does anyone have any ideas on how to make this idea more rigorous? Or is the set of assumption fundamentally contradictory?
comments.analysis: This comment thoughtfully questions the underlying assumptions of the dithering method and suggests directions for further optimization, showing a deep understanding of the topic.

comments.author: car
comments.text:
When the Mac and Atari ST first hit the market in the 80's, there were Comics created in this 1-bit "ordered-dither" style. For error-diffusion dithering (Floyd-Steinberg etc.), you needed more bits per pixel, to carry the error.

SHATTER:

https://imgur.com/gallery/shatter-1984-was-first-commerciall...

Robot Empire:

https://www.reddit.com/r/atarist/comments/xgs4rh/comicbook_c...
comments.analysis: Provides historical context about 1-bit dithering techniques and explains technical constraints, adding valuable background to the discussion.

comments.author: vagab0nd
comments.text:
This is great from a technical and artistic perspective. But for me personally, the visual style ruined a great game. I love detective/deduction games. I'm listing some of my all-time favorites in this genre. I'd love to finish Obra Dinn, but god it just makes my eyes hurt so much.

The Case of the Golden Idol

Chants of Sennaar

Her Story

IMMORTALITY

The Painscreek Killings

The Roottrees are Dead

Type Help
comments.analysis: Offers a personal and honest critique of the game's visual style, acknowledging its technical merits but expressing genuine discomfort, reflecting a common user experience.

comments.author: tesseractcat
comments.text: If anyone finds this interesting, I'd like to plug my post analyzing a similar technique, but generalized for perspective pixel art: https://tesseractc.at/shadowglass
comments.analysis: Shares a relevant resource that extends the discussion to related techniques, contributing constructively to the topic.

comments.author: mblode
comments.text:
I built a mini game with this technique [1][2]

[1] https://dither.blode.co

[2] https://github.com/mblode/dither-3d
comments.analysis: Provides practical application of the discussed technique, supporting the conversation with real-world implementation.

comments.author: paultendo
comments.text: What a fascinating deep dive. 2x with sphere mapping is my favourite - it starts to take on a sort of pointillism-like quality which gives all the objects (or maybe my brain) a sort of understanding of their texture.
comments.analysis: Reflects thoughtfully on the visual effect of the technique, offering an insightful artistic perspective.

comments.author: QuaternionsBhop
comments.text: The final dithering effect appears to be mis-sampled and is no longer 1 bit per pixel
comments.analysis: Raises a technical critique about the dithering output potentially violating the 1-bit constraint, which is relevant to the discussion.

comments.author: antics
comments.text:
> It feels a little weird to put 100 hours into something that won't be noticed by its absence. Exactly no one will think, "man this dithering is stable as shit. total magic going on here." I don't want to give people problems they didn't know they should have though so it was worth fixing.

I mean maybe it's just me, but that is literally the first thing I noticed and I appreciated it so much I instantly bought the game. I don't even play video games much!
comments.analysis: Expresses personal appreciation for the technical detail, highlighting how subtle technical achievements can impact user experience.

comments.author: progforlyfe
comments.text: I grew up on the small 6 inch 1 bit Mac SE display so the art style has a special place in my heart. Sadly I'm too "dumb" to fully enjoy the game as it requires a lot of attention to detail -- amazing if you enjoy detective style puzzles! I still highly respect it.
comments.analysis: Shares a nostalgic and respectful personal perspective, acknowledging the game's complexity and appeal to a niche audience.

comments.author: rokkamokka
comments.text: I gave this game a shot but honestly the art style got in the way of the gameplay for me. Fun to read how much effort went into it
comments.analysis: Provides a brief user experience critique focusing on the visual style's impact on gameplay enjoyment.
